Two types: Disease–progress curve Disease gradient curve --- ## Slide 59 Pattern and Rate of epidemic Disease Progress Disease on plants usually starts out at a low level, a small number of plants affected and a small amount of plant tissue affected, and it becomes of concern to us only when its incidence and severity increases with time. Progressive epidemic If the epidemic is considered as increase of diseases in extensity that is progressive epidemic. --- ## Slide 60 Disease Progress For example, in Phytophthora blight of pepper seedlings (Phytophthora capsici) and Fusarium kernel rot (Fusarium moniliforme) of maize, disease progress is roughly linear. --- ## Slide 61 Phytophthora blight of pepper seedlings ![Diagram: Slide 61 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s61_img1.png) Linear of disease progress --- ## Slide 62 Q Disease Progress Curve (DPC) Rate of epidemic per unit of time. Saturation type of curve Three monocyclic diseases of different epidemic rates. (B) Sigmoidal curve Polycyclic disease, such as late blight of potato. (C) Bimodal curve Polycyclic disease, such as apple scab, in which the blossoms and the fruit are infected at different, separate times --- ## Slide 63 ![Diagram: Slide 63 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s63_img1.jpg) Different Amount of initial inoculums different epidemic rate. A. Saturation type- for monocyclic disease --- ## Slide 64 ![Diagram: Slide 64 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s64_img1.jpg) Eg. Late blight of potato Three phase- log, exponential and lag phases. B. Sigmoid type- for polycyclic disease --- ## Slide 65 ![Diagram: Slide 65 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s65_img1.jpg) Disease affecting different organs C. Bimodal type --- ## Slide 66 Disease Gradient Curve (DGC) The percentage of disease and the scale for distance vary with the type of pathogen or its method of dispersal. Distance is small for soil borne pathogens or vectors and larger for airborne pathogens. The progress of an epidemic measured in terms of changes in the number of lesions, the amount of diseased tissue, and the number of diseased plants as it spreads over distance, is called disease gradient curve (spatial pattern). ![Diagram: Slide 66 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s66_img1.png) --- ## Slide 67 Rate curves It refers to the amount of increase of disease per unit time in the plant population. Rate curves are different types. which are as follows- Bell shaped Rate curve Symmetrical Rate curve Asymmetrical Rate curve --- ## Slide 68 ![Diagram: Slide 68 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s68_img1.jpg) Eg. Late blight of potato (Sigmoid Curve). Bell shaped Rate curve --- ## Slide 69 ![Diagram: Slide 69 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s69_img1.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 69 Image 2](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s69_img2.jpg) Eg. Apple scab (Bimodal Curve), mildew. Symmetrical Rate curve --- ## Slide 70 ![Diagram: Slide 70 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s70_img1.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 70 Image 2](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s70_img2.jpg) Eg. Alternaria leaf blight Asymmetrical Rate curve --- ## Slide 71 Relationships between disease cycles and epidemics Pathogens that complete their life cycle in one year are called monocyclic Fungi such as smut produce spores at the end of the season to begin the next season Root rot and vascular wilts require two seasons and alternate hosts to complete their life cycle --- ## Slide 72 Relationships between disease cycles and epidemics Some pathogens go through many generations in one year They are called polycyclic pathogens They can complete from 2 to 30 cycles in per year Polycyclic pathogens are usually airborne or airborne vectors and can cause explosive epidemics --- ## Slide 73 Relationships between disease cycles and epidemics Downy mildew, late blight of potato, powdery mildews, leaf spots and blights, grain rusts and insect-borne viruses cause explosive epidemics The number of spores etc that survive the season is small but multiply rapidly once conditions are suitable --- ## Slide 74 Relationships between disease cycles and epidemics In some diseases of trees, fungal vascular wilts, phytoplasmal declines, and viral infections the pathogen may not complete the life cycle in one year but may take several years to produce inoculum. These are called polyetic. Sine they remain in the host, they produce exponentially. (Dutch elm disease) --- ## Slide 75 Comparison of Disease Cycles | | Fungi | Bacteria | Viruses | Nematodes | |---|---|---|---|---| | Survival | Crop residue, Soil, Alternate hosts | Crop residues, Soil, Alternate hosts, Insect vectors | Alternate hosts, Insect vectors | Crop residues, Soil | | Dispersal | Wind Rain Insects | Wind Rain Insects | Insects | Tillage Equipment Water run-off | | Infection | Directly Wounds Insect feeding | Wounds Insect feeding | Insect feeding | Directly | 75 --- ---

# Lecture: 3.1 Methods of Plant Disease Control # Course Slides: 3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control ## Slide 1 Methods of Plant Disease Control --- ## Slide 2 What is Control Measures…… Measures taken to prevent the incidence of disease, reduce the amount of inoculum, and minimize the loss are known as control measures. Plant disease management is an eminent process in crop production. A disease can be managed by eliminating interaction between A susceptible host A virulent pathogen In suitable environmental condition --- ## Slide 3 Q --- ## Slide 4 Disease control There are 6 basic types of control measures: a. Biological control b. Cultural control c. Legislative control Chemical control Physical control Host resistance --- ## Slide 5 Q PRINCIPLES OF PLANT DISEASE MANAGEMENT 1) AVOIDANCE 2) EXCLUSION 3) ERADICATION 4) PROTECTION 5) RESISTANT VARIETIES 6) THERAPY --- ## Slide 6 Avoidance Q Avoiding disease by altering planting time, or planting in areas where inoculum is ineffective due to environmental conditions. Avoidance can be carried out by:- | Choice of geographical areas. | |---| | Choice of time of showing. | | Selection of seed and planting material. | | Selection of field. | | Disease escaping varieties. | | Modification of cultural practices. | --- ## Slide 7 Q Exclusion Preventing the inoculum from entering or establishing in the field. Seed treatment Inspection Certification Quarantine (Federal or State) Cleaned farm equipment ![Diagram: Slide 7 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s7_img1.jpg) Cleaning farm equipment --- ## Slide 8 Q Eradication Aimed at eliminating a pathogen after it is introduced into an area but before it has widely spread. It can be applied to Individual plants, Seed lots, Fields or regions. It is generally not effective over large geographic areas. --- ## Slide 9 Q Eradiation can be done by…… Destroying weeds that are reservoirs of various pathogens or insect vectors of disease, Biological control of plant pathogen Crop rotation Soil treatment Heat and chemical treatment ![Diagram: Slide 9 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s9_img1.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 9 Image 2](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s9_img2.jpg) Elimination of potato cull piles Weed Control --- ## Slide 10 ![Diagram: Slide 10 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s10_img1.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 10 Image 2](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s10_img2.jpg) Crop Rotation Burning of infected residues crop ![Diagram: Slide 10 Image 3](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s10_img3.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 10 Image 4](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s10_img4.jpg) Propane flaming Soil Treatment --- ## Slide 11 Q Protection Preventing infection by creating a chemical toxic barrier between the plants and pathogens. Chemical treatment Chemical control of insect vector Modification of environment Modification of host nutrition ![Diagram: Slide 11 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s11_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 12 Protection can be done by…… ![Diagram: Slide 12 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s12_img1.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 12 Image 2](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s12_img2.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 12 Image 3](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s12_img3.jpg) Bananas are covered with plastic sleeves Ground sprayers Raised planting beds --- ## Slide 13 Q Resistant Varieties Preventing infection or reducing the effect of infection by managing the host through improvement of resistance in it by genetic manipulation or by chemotherapy. Selection and hybridization of disease resistance Mutation of disease resistance ![Diagram: Slide 13 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s13_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 14 ![Diagram: Slide 14 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s14_img1.jpg) Susceptible and resistant plant ![Diagram: Slide 14 Image 2](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s14_img2.jpg) Susceptible and resistant plant to papaya ringspot virus --- ## Slide 15 Q Therapy of disease plant Reducing the severity of disease in an infected individual by chemicals. Chemotherapy Tree Surgery Heat Therapy Ex: Bordeaux Mixture. ![Diagram: Slide 15 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s15_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 16 Cultural control Q Cultural control: Manipulation of the environment to control diseases caused by pathogens. There are many types of cultural control. Here are a few selected examples: Crop rotation — rotate crops and varieties over seasons to reduce pathogen inoculum levels. This is probably the most widely employed control measure in agriculture. example: Rotate soybean with corn to control soybean cyst nematode --- ## Slide 17 Cultural control Q Selection of planting date or planting location — Choose a time/place favorable for the host, rather than the pathogen: avoid pathogen or its vector example: (Time) Plant cotton late to control damping-off caused by Pythium (warm soil) Seeding rate and canopy density — Adjust within-row and between-row spacing to open the canopy and reduce diseases that spread in the humid, protected canopy environment --- ## Slide 18 Cultural control Q Irrigation Pathogens can be spread in irrigation water or favored by wet soils-example: late blight (Phytophthora) Pathogens can be controlled by flooding – for example: Fusarium wilt on banana Control insects and weeds — Insect vectors, viruses, other pathogens, and weeds serve as alternate hosts for pathogens or vectors and increase canopy density --- ## Slide 19 Cultural control Q Sanitation Keep the area free of diseased plant material by pruning diseased branches (fireblight), plowing under or burning debris, and washing and sterilizing harvesting and processing equipment (Rhizopus soft rot); poor sanitation contributed to the late blight outbreak that caused the Irish famine Balanced fertilizer application and proper tillage – Balanced fertilizer application is treated as a control measure to kill or inhibit the pathogens in the field. --- ## Slide 20 Role of Trap Crops and Cover Crops in Disease Management Q ![Diagram: Slide 20 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s20_img1.png) --- ## Slide 21 Role of Trap Crops and Cover Crops in Disease Management Q ![Diagram: Slide 21 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s21_img1.png) --- ## Slide 22 ![Diagram: Slide 22 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s22_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 23 Legislative control Quarantine — detention and associated practices for preventing the entry of diseased materials or pathogens into an area; relatively inexpensive; can be at the national or state level Plant Quarantine Act of Bangladesh, 2011 An Act to make provisions, in the context of international traffic in plants and plant products, for preventing the introduction of insects or pests into, and spread thereof within, Bangladesh and for the matters relating to phytosanitary and other measures incidental and ancillary thereto. --- ## Slide 24 Main activities of plant quarantine  Tracing, identification and control of quarantine pests. Pest risk analysis, evaluation and determination of quarantine conditions for imported and transit plant shipments. Inspection, sampling, testing and undertaking quarantine regulations on imported and transit agricultural shipments. Supervision and undertaking post-entry quarantine regulations on plants which are subject to the regulations. Inspection, sampling, testing and issuing phytosanitary certificate for exporting plant consignments. --- ## Slide 25 Host Resistance --- ## Slide 26 1. Host resistance - control based on the genes and the resistance mechanisms they control Van der Plank described two types of resistance (the 1960s; these are the "classics‘’): Vertical resistance — resistance that is effective against some, but not all, races of a pathogen; decreases the effective amount of incoming inoculum (avirulent races can't infect), but does not reduce the rate of disease development (virulent races are not affected) Horizontal resistance — resistance that is effective against all races of the pathogen; decreases the rate of disease development for all races --- ## Slide 27 ![Diagram: Slide 27 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s27_img1.wmf) --- ## Slide 28 b. Resistance has been defined in many other ways since Van der Plank, including systems based on: epidemiologic effects, number of genes involved, how long the resistance lasts under field conditions; additional terms you should be familiar with are: tolerance — plants are diseased, but they do not yield less than healthy plants induced resistance — a normally susceptible plant treated with an avirulent strain of a pathogen gives a resistant reaction when challenged later with a strain that is virulent --- ## Slide 29 Physical Methods Heat : Elevated temperature (42 to 50oC) usually beneficial, but leads to greater loss of seed germination. Hot water treatment: Pre-soaking the seed Pre-heating at 100C for 1 to 2 min Hot water soak Drying Cooling Hot air treatment Refrigeration Solar heat treatment --- ## Slide 30 Combinations – Mung Bean Seed Dry heat (50oC, 1 h) no effect on germination and sprout growth rate Dry heat (50oC, 1 h) followed by gamma irradiation (2.0 kGy) → no effect on germination, reduced sprout growth rate --- ## Slide 31 Hot Water – Mung Bean Seed Hot water 55oC/20 min 60oC/10 min 70oC/5 min 80oC/2 min None of the treatments reduced seed germination below 95%. Thicker seed coat protects the embryo. --- ## Slide 32 Protocol for Tomato Seed: Preheat seed for 10 min. at 100°F (37°C). Transfer to 122°F (50°C) for 25 min. Cool seed immediately and dry completely prior to sowing or re-packaging. Seed may be treated with fungicidal dust when dry. Hot-Water Seed Treatment From S.A. Miller, Ohio St. Univ. ![Diagram: Slide 32 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s32_img1.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 32 Image 2](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s32_img2.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 32 Image 3](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s32_img3.jpg) --- ## Slide 33 ![Diagram: Slide 33 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s33_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 34 ![Diagram: Slide 34 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s34_img1.png) --- ## Slide 35 ![Diagram: Slide 35 Image 1](/uploads/3.1 Method of Plant Disease Control_s35_img1.png) Soil solarization The soil surface is covered with transparent plastic film, taking advantage of solar radiation to heat the soil to temperatures that are lethal to many fungal pathogens and nematodes. Soil steam sterilization Soil in open fields or greenhouses is sterilized using steam. The steam kills plant pathogens (bacteria, fungi, viruses) by causing vital cellular proteins to denature. --- ## Slide 36 Flooding Temporary flooding is used as an effective method to control plant-parasitic nematodes and other soilborne pathogens. Burning crop and pruning residues For diseases for which no cure exists, infected crop and pruning residues are burned as a last resort option. Burning is an effective control of all kinds of pathogens --- ---
# Lecture: 3.2 Biological Control of Plant Diseases # Course Slides: 3.2 Biological Control ## Slide 1 Biological control of plant pathogens Christine Roath --- ## Slide 2 Overview What is biological control, what are the benefits to its use Mechanism of biological control Requirements of successful biocontrol Working example of biocontrol --- ## Slide 3 What is biological control? First coined by Harry Smith in relation to the biological control of insects Suppression of insect populations by native or introduced enemies Generic terms A population-leveling process in which the population of one species lowers the number of another --- ## Slide 4 Why use biological control? WHEN : Biological control agents are Expensive Labor intensive Host specific WHILE : Chemical pesticides are: cost-effective easy to apply Broad spectrum --- ## Slide 5 Q Why use biological control? WILL: Chemical pesticides Implicated in ecological, environmental, and human health problems Require yearly treatments Broad spectrum Toxic to both beneficial and pathogenic species BUT: Biological control agents Non-toxic to human Not a water contaminant concern Once colonized may last for years Host specific Only effect one or few species --- ## Slide 6 ![Diagram: Slide 6 Image 1](/uploads/3.2 Biological Control_s6_img1.png) Q --- ## Slide 7 Q ![Diagram: Slide 7 Image 1](/uploads/3.2 Biological Control_s7_img1.png) --- ## Slide 8 Q Antagonism Definition: Antagonism refers to the inhibitory relationship where one organism suppresses or harms another, often by competition, production of antibiotics, or other mechanisms. Example: The bacterium Bacillus subtilis produces antibiotics that inhibit the growth of the fungal pathogen Fusarium oxysporum, which causes wilt in many crops. --- ## Slide 9 Q Antagonist Definition: An antagonist is the organism that suppresses or inhibits the growth or activity of a pest or pathogen through various mechanisms like competition, parasitism, or production of harmful substances. Example: Trichoderma harzianum is a fungal antagonist that suppresses the growth of plant-pathogenic fungi like Rhizoctonia solani by outcompeting them for nutrients and space or by directly attacking them. --- ## Slide 10 Mechanisms of biological control of plant pathogens/ How do antagonists act? Q Antibiosis – Inhibition of one organism by another as a result of diffusion of an antibiotic Antibiotic production common in soil-dwelling bacteria and fungi Example: zwittermicin A production by B. cereus against Phytophthora root rot in alfalfa --- ## Slide 11 Q Mechanisms of biological control of plant pathogens Nutrient competition – competition between microorganisms for carbon, nitrogen, O2, iron, and other nutrients Most common way organisms limit growth of others Example P. fluorescens, VITCUS, prevents bacterial blotch by competing with P. tolaasii --- ## Slide 12 Q Mechanisms of biological control of plant pathogens Destructive mycoparasitism – the parasitism of one fungus by another Direct contact Cell wall degrading enzymes Some produce antibiotics Example Trichoderma harzianum, BioTrek, used as seed treatment against pathogenic fungus --- ## Slide 13 Q ![Diagram: Slide 13 Image 1](/uploads/3.2 Biological Control_s13_img1.png) ![Diagram: Slide 13 Image 2](/uploads/3.2 Biological Control_s13_img2.png) --- ## Slide 14 Q ![Diagram: Slide 14 Image 1](/uploads/3.2 Biological Control_s14_img1.png) ![Diagram: Slide 14 Image 2](/uploads/3.2 Biological Control_s14_img2.png) ![Diagram: Slide 14 Image 3](/uploads/3.2 Biological Control_s14_img3.png) --- ## Slide 16 Requirements of successful biocontrol Inexpensive production and formulation of agent must be developed Production must result in biomass with excellent shelf live To be successful as agricultural agent must be Inexpensive Able to produce in large quantities Maintain viability --- ## (Plant Biocontrol by Trichoderma spp. Ilan Chet, Ada Viterbo and Yariv Brotman) --- ## Slide 18 Plant pathogen control by Trichoderma spp. Trichoderma spp. are present in nearly all agricultural soils Antifungal abilities have been known since 1930s Mycoparasitism Nutrient competition Agriculturally used as biocontrol agent and as a plant growth promoter | | |---| ![Diagram: Slide 18 Image 1](/uploads/3.2 Biological Control_s18_img1.jpg) http://www.ars.usda.gov/is/pr/2002/021231.trichoderma.jpg --- ## Slide 19 Plant pathogen control by Trichoderma spp. Why buy/develop a product that is readily available in the soil? --- ## Slide 20 Plant pathogen control by Trichoderma spp. Genetic Modification Wild strains Heterokaryotic – contain nuclei of dissimilar genotypes within a single organism Biocontrol strains Homokaryotic – contain nuclei which are similar or identical Allows genetic distinction and non-variability IMPORTANT FOR QUALITY CONTROL --- ## Slide 21 Plant pathogen control by Trichoderma spp. Most strains have innate resistance to some agricultural chemicals Resistance is variable Strains available for commercial use are selected or modified for resistance to specific chemicals --- ## Slide 22 Plant pathogen control by Trichoderma spp. How is it applied? # Lecture: 3.3 Chemical Control & Fungicides # Course Slides: 3.3 Chemical Control ## Slide 1 Chemicals Used in Plants Disease Control ![Diagram: Slide 1 Image 1](/uploads/3.3 Chemical Control_s1_img1.png) --- ## Slide 2 Use of chemical in plant disease control Designed to control plant diseases by inhibiting the growth of or by killing the disease-causing pathogens. Prevent or reduce infections by utilizing various principles of disease control. ![Diagram: Slide 2 Image 1](/uploads/3.3 Chemical Control_s2_img1.png) --- ## Slide 3 History ![Diagram: Slide 3 Image 1](/uploads/3.3 Chemical Control_s3_img1.png) --- ## Slide 4 History Prior to 1882: Disease control with element sulfur and copper From 1882 to 1934: Disease control based on organo-metalics (fixed or organo-copper) 1934: Modern era of organic fungicides began with the dithiocarbamates 1943: Greatly improved fungicidal activity --- ## Slide 5 Chemical control --- ## Slide 6 Chemical used in plant disease control --- ## Slide 7 Fungicides Fungicide Mobility Any toxic substance used to kill or inhibit the growth and reproduction of fungi is called fungicides. Apoplastic movement- Xylem- mobile fungicides are absorbed by the plant and translocated upward to other tissues. Symplastic Movement downward, with carbohydrates in the phloem. Amphimobile fungicides move throughout the plant. Translaminar fungicides can move across and through the leaves, but are not truly systemic, that travels only shorter distances in the plant. --- ## Slide 8 What is apoplastic and symplastic transport mechanism? The symplast pathway involves protoplasts, which are found within the living cytoplasm cells. Eg. Carbendazim The apoplast pathway is a non-living route since the water solution moves in the spaces between cells and along the cell wall (no protoplasts involved). Eg. Benomyl (Bengimidazol) Fungicide Mobility ![Diagram: Slide 8 Image 1](/uploads/3.3 Chemical Control_s8_img1.png) --- ## Slide 9 What is the difference between Translaminar and systemic? Translaminar control agents penetrate into the plant tissue and are moved within a plant organ such as a leaf, but do not travel to other parts of the plant. For example, the chemical may move from the upper to lower surface of a leaf. Systemic control agents can be effective at sites other than where they are sprayed. Fungicide Mobility ![Diagram: Slide 9 Image 1](/uploads/3.3 Chemical Control_s9_img1.png) --- ## Slide 10 1. Apoplastic Movement (acropetal): Move upward from the point of entry through the plant’s xylem. e.g. Tebuconazole, Propamocarb, Benomyl. 4. Translaminar fungicides (locally systemic): Absorbed by leaves and move through the leaf to the opposite surface. e.g. Dimethomorph, Iprodione, etc. 2. Symplastic Movement (basipetal): Move downward, with carbohydrates in the phloem. e.g. Trifloxystrobin, Carbendazim. 3. Amphimobile fungicides (truly systemic): Move both upward through the xylem, and downward through the phloem. e.g. Fosetyl Al, Phosphoric acid. --- ## Slide 11 Inhibit fungal growth kill fungal pathogens Prevent damage caused by fungi Inhibit spore production --- ## Slide 12 Fungicides Chemical nature Mode of action General use --- ## Slide 13 Classification of fungicides based on chemical nature Sulphur : zineb, maneb Copper: bordeaux mixture Mercurial: phenyl mercury acetate Quinone: dichlone Heterocyclic nitrogenous: captan Oxathiins: carboxin Benzimidazoles: benomyl MBC Fungicides in plant disease control, Y.L.
